standard_description: Understand a fraction 1/b as the quantity formed by 1 part when a whole is partitioned into b equal parts; understand a fraction a/b as the quantity formed by a parts of size 1/b.
sources: Empson, S.B. & Levi, L. (2011). Extending Children's Mathematics: Fractions and Decimals. Heinemann.
NCTM Progressions for the Common Core State Standards in Mathematics — Number and Operations: Fractions, 3–5, 2013
Van de Walle, J.A., Karp, K.S., & Bay-Williams, J.M. (2013). Elementary and Middle School Mathematics (8th ed.). Pearson.
EngageNY Grade 3 Module 5 Topic A — Partitioning a Whole into Equal Parts, 2013
Smarter Balanced Assessment Consortium — Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications, 2015

misconceptions.description: Student counts pieces of a shape and writes a fraction without checking equal size. For a shape cut into 4 unequal parts, says 'each is 1/4.' The 'equal parts' requirement is missed.
misconceptions.example: A pizza cut into 4 unequal slices. Student says each slice is 1/4. Correct: cannot be called 1/4 unless all slices are equal.
misconceptions.citation: Empson & Levi (2011) p.23 — 'The most fundamental fraction misconception is acceptance of unequal partitions; insisting on equal parts before naming any fraction prevents this from becoming entrenched.'
misconceptions.remediation: Always check: 'Are all parts the same size?' before naming. Use overlap test: cut out one part — does it match the others? Refuse fraction names for unequal partitions.

misconceptions.description: Student sees N pieces and writes 1/N as the fraction for one piece, regardless of whether the pieces are equal. The connection 'fraction = equal share' is incomplete.
misconceptions.example: A rectangle cut into 5 different-sized pieces. Student says 'one of these is 1/5' — but only if all 5 were equal would 1/5 apply.
misconceptions.citation: EngageNY G3 Module 5 Topic A Teacher Edition — 'Equal-piece reasoning is the gateway to fraction understanding; without it students treat fraction names as decorative labels rather than mathematical statements about shares.'
misconceptions.remediation: Connect denominator to '_ equal parts make the whole.' Verify: do N copies of one piece exactly cover the whole? If yes → 1/N. If no → not a fraction yet.

misconceptions.short_label: Confuses fraction names (thirds vs fourths vs sixths)
misconceptions.description: Student knows the shape has equal parts but uses the wrong fraction word. Says 'fourths' for 3 equal parts (correct: thirds) or 'thirds' for 4 equal parts (correct: fourths).
misconceptions.example: A circle cut into 3 equal slices. Student says 'each slice is one-fourth.' Correct: one-third.
misconceptions.citation: Van de Walle et al. (2013) p.293 — 'Fraction-name confusion is a vocabulary error that masquerades as a conceptual one; explicit teaching of the count-name correspondence (2=halves, 3=thirds, 4=fourths…) resolves it.'
misconceptions.source: Van de Walle Elementary and Middle School Mathematics
misconceptions.remediation: Memorize the count-name pairs: 2 parts = halves, 3 = thirds, 4 = fourths, 5 = fifths, 6 = sixths, 7 = sevenths, 8 = eighths. Match name to count of parts.

misconceptions.short_label: Identifies parts but loses the whole
misconceptions.description: Student names the parts (e.g., 'fourths') but cannot say what the whole was. Treats fractions as isolated pieces rather than parts-of-a-whole.
misconceptions.example: Student given 3 squares (each 1/4 of a hidden whole). Asked 'what fraction?' answers '3 squares' or '3/3' — missing that they are 3/4 of one larger whole.
misconceptions.citation: NCTM NF Progressions 3-5 (2013) p.4 — 'The whole is the conceptual anchor of all fraction work; students who lose track of the whole produce technically named but mathematically incoherent fraction expressions.'
misconceptions.remediation: Always ask first: 'What is the WHOLE?' before naming any fraction. Draw a box around the whole shape. Then count: how many equal parts? How many shaded?

misconceptions.short_label: Thinks different shapes mean different fractions
misconceptions.description: Student sees a square cut into 4 strips and a square cut into 4 small squares — and says they are different fractions because the pieces look different. Misses that 1/4 of the whole is 1/4 regardless of part shape.
misconceptions.example: Two squares both 1/4-shaded but in different patterns. Student says 'left is 1/4 but right is 1/8' based on visual perception, not equal partition.
misconceptions.citation: Empson & Levi (2011) p.31 — 'Equal-area independence of part-shape is non-obvious; students must learn that 1/4 means "one of four equal-area parts," regardless of how those parts are arranged.'
misconceptions.remediation: Show two different partitions of the same fraction (e.g., 1/4 as a strip vs 1/4 as a corner). Verify equal area by overlapping cut-outs. Reinforce: fraction = relative size, not visual shape.

misconceptions.short_label: Confuses equal pieces with equal area
misconceptions.description: Student counts pieces and accepts them as equal because there are N of them, even when the pieces have visibly different areas. Or rejects equal partitions because pieces are different shapes.
misconceptions.example: A square divided into a small triangle and a large rectangle. Student says '2 parts, so halves.' Correct: not halves — areas unequal.
misconceptions.citation: EngageNY G3 Module 5 Topic A Teacher Edition — 'Visual partition does not imply mathematical equality; students must apply the area-equality test, not the count-equality test.'
misconceptions.remediation: Use grid paper or overlap pieces to check equal area. 'Equal' in fractions means equal AREA, not just equal count of pieces.

misconceptions.short_label: Pieces don't cover the whole — gaps or overlap
misconceptions.description: Student accepts a 'partition' that has gaps or overlapping pieces. The pieces don't fully cover (or over-cover) the whole, so they are not valid equal parts.
misconceptions.example: A rectangle with 4 equal pieces drawn but one piece is missing — only 3 pieces visible. Student says 'fourths.' Correct: not valid until all 4 pieces are present and cover the whole.
misconceptions.citation: Van de Walle et al. (2013) p.294 — 'Complete-covering is a prerequisite for fraction naming; partial partitions or overlapping pieces produce invalid fractional descriptions.'
misconceptions.source: Van de Walle Elementary and Middle School Mathematics
misconceptions.remediation: Verify: do the pieces FILL the whole exactly? No gaps and no overlaps? Only then can fraction names apply.
